turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in No. 79-1171, Minnesota v. Clover Leaf. Gentlemen, since we have only about four minutes left we will not ask you to split your argument. We will take your case up at 1 o'clock, after the lunch hour.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Raschke, you.may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The State of Minnesota is here today asking this Court to review and reverse a decision of the Minnesota Supreme Court which struck down supposedly on equal protection grounds a Minnesota law which would have stopped the sale at retail in Minnesota of milk in single-use throwaway bottles which are made 100 percent from oil and natural gas resources. In reality, however, this case deals with the ques­ under the banner of substantive due process interposed their particular piece of legislation will best do the job set out for it. The statute in question was proposed by the Minnesota. Pollution Control Agency and passed by the Legislature in the spring of 1977 as an implementation of general State policy and additionally as a reaction to a specific set of circum­ stances which were then existing within the State. In general, the agency and the Legislature recognizee the ridiculous waste of resources and energy, and particularly fossil fuels, in many circumstances, as well as the unnecessary

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: This bill originally was introduced in the form of banning specifically the plastic jug in reaction to a then-pending move by major dairies within the State to commit a lot of money and capital expenditures in long-term commitments to the throwaway plastic jug. At one point in time there was an amendment proposed that would have banned paper but it passed in the form origi­ nally introduced banning only the plastic jug. I think the idea was to wait and see what sort of effect this Act would have had. industry in Minnesota both have clout, and -- I guess my ques­ tion is, how political this is, rather than how environmentally protective ?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Well, I don't think it was necessarily at all political in the sense of reacting without legitimate policy to specific pressure groups. I think it was clear from reacting to, as I indicated in my prior answer, is that the milk industry, major dairies within the State, were on the verge of spending hundreds of thousands of dollars in commit­ ment to long-term production of these plastic jugs, and the machinery necessary to produce these plastic jugs is both ex­ pensive and has a long useful life.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Production within the State?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Production within the State; that's true. It was recognized that the paper container is not the environmental ideal. The paper container became common during this move to plastic would lock us into a situation which would the tone of the legislative hearing. There were copious amounts of data, government reports, which were used -- the same reports used by both sides in supporting their positions. Opinions, expert and non-expert alike , were offered on the question of whether the plastic jug is the single worst container from an environmental standpoint or merely the second-worst container possible for the sale of milk. Specula­ tion and.ideas were offered concerning whether the milk indus­ try would or would not have a greater tendency to go to pre­ ferable containers with or without the plastic jug. And when

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: General Raschke, does the record tell us what happened during the last three years? Did they all con­ vert to plastic?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: During the last three years while this case was in litigation, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: I don't believe the record reflects that at all. I'm not aware of anything in the record which guess they've been permitted to do, then the reason for the was to prevent the situation in which the industry was permit­ ted to go ahead and make these types of investments and then later complain of the tremendous impact it would have on them to then come back and ban either the plastic or the paper. I don't think that has significance in viewing the rational basis of the Legislature. Otherwise any attempt of this sort could be defeated by the regulated industry running out, making the commitments, and by defeating themselves in attempting to defeat the legislative purpose. these containers, I don't see any reason why they don't enact the second statute.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Well, I think the goal of the Legisla­ ture, Your Honor, is to proceed a step at a time beginning with the least intrusive sort of regulation. Now, this statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, the least offensive regulation is to let the paper containers continue forever, I suppose.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: In -- well, in moving in the direction of our goal, this statute has never been permitted to take sort of effect that we hope for it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, you can't take the next step until this case is resolved, is that your point?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: That's perhaps --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would remove any constitutional ques­ tion and it would clearly accomplish your environmental goal. The fact that you don't do it makes one wonder whether that's really the purpose of the legislation.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: There are a number of other factors. Both the parties here that have been selling milk in plastic containers and other dairies, most other dairies in the State of Minnesota, are selling milk in paper containers, either paper containers in addition to plastic or paper containers ture could well have chosen to first half plastic and hope So, hopefully, if we remove the greatest single com­ petitive enemy to more environmentally sound containers, there may be some voluntary movement in that area. And I think that's a judgment that the Legislature is entitled to make. Certainly the option that you suggest and the option that was suggested by the Minnesota Supreme Court would more quickly accomplish the environmental goal, but I think that it's at the expense of other things. I think the past decisions of this Court in many cases permits the Legislature to choose a step at a time situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I interrupt you for a minute?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: You may.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You make the argument that the Minnesota findings ?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: I think there are a couple of problems, many problems, with the findings of the District Court. I think the problem with the District Court was that it clearly said that it felt it was its job to review, weigh, and balance the evidence, because the District Court saw itself as the initial fact finder. The Minnesota Supreme Court, first of all, did not I believe adopt the finding that the Act had a protectionist motive. I think the Supreme Court, our Supreme Court noted at A-35, I think it is, in the Appendix, that the rejecting the theory of the trial court. But a problem that our Supreme Court shared with the beginning, "We are aware." On A-5?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Petition for certiorari?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Oh, petition for cert. I was looking at the Appendix. A-5?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's the paragraph beginning, "We are aware"?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The third sentence: "Based upon the that specifically the stated legislative finding of the Act is that nonrefillable milk containers present solid waste prob­ lems and the stated legislative goal is that the use of returnable milk containers should be encouraged? And then it said, "The Act, undoubtedly, deals with legitimate State in­ terests . "

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Yes, it did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that inconsistent with the trial court's -- I don't think by affirming, by saying that they were generally affirming the trial court's standards that they were getting to that point. The important thing is that our court after having said this, (a) was looking at its function as reviewing the trial court's findings; and (b) undertook the task of determining which of admittedly admissible evidence was more or less credible than others, in making the ultimate conclu­ sions about which type of regulation would be best effective and what the court felt was the single worst container. It's clear that on a proper form of analysis that the Act must shows that it's the greatest waster of fossil fuel energy and will take up the most space in landfills. It's the greatest competitive barrier to better containers. We're on the verge of a -- we're on the verge of long-term commitments to that. If we add the competitive advantage together with these long­ term commitments and the incentive to produce and promote the throwaway container that that would entail, we would virtually guarantee no movement towards the goal of better containers. The plaintiffs here, as we've been discussing pre­ viously, have sought to make the Commerce Clause --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't understand that last argument guaranteeing it. All they have to do is ban the other con­ tainer too, don't they?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: We could do that, Your Honor, and I think that would be a legitimate legislative choice, but what we're arguing here is that it is not a constitutionally man­ dated legislative choice. Cases that this Court has .decided people who were then in business would go out of business. But there's nothing in this record to say that you're ever go­ ing to prohibit paper containers.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Well, I think in the legislative --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is no one step at a time argument. It's just one step, period, is what you're saying. You're going to take one step with no desire to take the other step.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: I think the Legislature can be allowed to take the first step without being constitutionally required to guarantee when that second step will occur. ever intends to take the second step.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: In the Railway Express case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's your position, isn't it?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: In the Railway Express case this Court indicated that the legislature is free to address one portion of the problem and leave other portions for a later time.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: I guess the Legislature ought to be entitled after this case is over and hopefully this Court woulc reverse the Minnesota decision and allow this step to take ef­ fect to see how less restrictive, less onerous regulations work before we're constitutionally required by court decree to take the most intrusive measure in accomplishing the goal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, so, has the result of this case been that plastics are now being used?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: They are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the rationale has disappeared now for saying that we started with plastics because they really hadn't come in yet?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Well, we're trying to stop the major financial commitments, for one thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's past now, isn't it?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: We are not aware that those commitments have been made. I'm not aware, the record does not indicate is sale taking place in plastic, but as I said before, if we're

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Your time has expired now. You're cutting into your colleague's time.

turns.name: RASHKE
turns.text: I've granted ten minutes to Mr. Dalton of the Solicitor's Office and if he has any time left I would like to rebut.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Mr. Dalton.

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I propose to first explain the Federal Government's interest in and participation in this case; and secondly, to capsulize more briefly than I had expected where the Minnesota Supreme Court went wrong. And then I will turn to the Commerce Clause issues that I think are behind Mr. Justice Powell's questions. The federal interest in this case is really two-fold, event plastic can be recycled. Therefore there was no need to give serious attention to the problem of depletion of non-re- newable fuels. The problem is that if you multiply the oil and gas used in milk cartons in Minnesota across 50 states, a cross­ patching not only for milk but for other beverages and food­ stuffs and over time, then the problem may indeed be substan­ tial . As for the question of the recycling of plastic, there is nothing in the evidence to indicate that the plastic or that recycling is feasible. The Legislature certainly could have concluded to the contrary, and in any event, even if recycling of plastic was a way of reducing the dependence on states and see what the states' experience would be in various methods directed to beverage container disposal. Now, that proposal was directed to beer and soft drink containers but the same notion of using the states as a laboratory applies with equal force to milk containers. Indeed not only is it in the Federal Government’s interest to use the states' experimentations in this area as a laboratory, it's in the interest of each of the individual states including Minnesota to take a step and look empirically at their exper­ The experience of Vermont in this respect, we find, very instructive, and that’s detailed in Footnote 6 of our brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Dalton, can I ask you, on this ques­ tion of federal interest, if there is a choice presented in any commerce situation between using plastic containers and preference ?

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: I think the Federal Government has a very clear preference for returnable bottles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that but if there's a choice between two disposables, one paper and one plastic, does the Federal Government have an interest in one over the other?

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Only to the extent that plastic does use nonrenewable fossil fuels. To that extent. Assuming the paper and plastic --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then your answer is, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You do. There is a federal interest in preferring plastic disposable containers over paper disposable containers ? environmentally disruptive. The State of Minnesota's Legislature has made that judgment --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand, but I'm curious about the federal interest in this particular issue, and is there a federal interest in preferring plastic over paper in disposable containers? I'm not quite clear on whether you've answered this or not.

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Seems to me it's a yes or no question.

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Well, in that case the answer must be, fuels.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you have the same interest if a neighboring state said, we will allow plastic but not paper, because we think that's environmentally superior? Would you also say that choice should be defended in the same rationale?

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Yes, we would have an interest in that statute because we have an interest in seeing whether that process is an appropriate one or a successful one in leading

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Precisely. I’d like to speak briefly to the question of pulp and the fact that there is some evi­ dence in the record -- at least, statements by legislators -- that this bill is related to the interests of the timber indus­ try in Minnesota. First off, this Court has recognized in the United States v. O'Brien that legislation which on its face is consti- -tutional ought not to be struck down because some legislators have made statements without which the statute would survive. Secondly, it’s not a sufficient criticism to say that: supposed to take into account the political, that is the economic and the social interests of their citizens. What they can't do is to place burdens on interstate commerce that vio­ late the Commerce Clause. Our position is that this statute does not do that. This statute is not protectionist on its

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are not saying, I am sure, that the legislative purpose would prevent any factual inquiry as to whether or not the effect of this statute really was discrimi­ natory against interstate commerce, are you?

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: No, I'm certainly not saying that by simply enunciating what legislative purposes that seem consistent with the Commerce Clause, a state can insulate that statute from Commerce Clause challenge. But what I am saying is that in this case there is no evidence that there was in fact a protectionist purpose and -- trary .

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: But our position is that the Supreme Court --

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: Again, please? merce. What the statute does is prohibit a product, not the product manufactured at a particular location.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are there any plastic manufacturers in Minnesota?

turns.name: DALTON
turns.text: My understanding is that there were not at the time that the statute was enacted, but that there was a move afoot to have in-state manufacturing of plastic cartons. That's part of what motivated the statute. That's part of the capital investment to which Mr. Raschke referred that indeed Well, I'll cede the balance of my time back to the petitioner.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Keyes.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court: The Court today has before it a unique Minnesota statute. It's a criminal act that hasn't been adopted by any other legislature in the United States. It affects only milk. Milk cannot be packaged or sold at retail in Minnesota in a brief to throwaway bottles. The Solicitor General in his brief talks about the anti-littering statutes which have been held constitutional throughout this country. This is not an anti­ littering statute on either side. Nobody drinks milk and throws the bottle out the window, whether it's a plastic bottle or a paperboard bottle. Therefore, the question is simply --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sooner or later, Mr. Keyes, someone must dispose of the container, whatever it is, must they not?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Surely. Surely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It may even take about four drinks but it is --

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: They don't litter it, Your Honor. They do not litter the container. That's our position. Obviously, it has to be disposed of some way, either by recycling, by combustion, or by landfill, are the basic —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And are these plastic containers combusti­ ble? Minnesota right now. Furthermore, this is not a novel product for Minne­ sota --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you agree that the trial was much like a legislative hearing?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: I do not agree with that, Mr. Justice Marshall. There was evidence produced. There was evidence produced which the court found preponderant, rated overwhelm­ ingly in favor of the plaintiffs. Obviously there was evidence produced at the trial; that was what the trial was for. There ings. But I would say that the result of the lower Minnesota court's decision, keeping in mind that the Minnesota court un­ derstands those principles on which it must decide these consti­ tutional issues, found that the plaintiffs had sustained the tremendous burden which is placed upon them in attacking the other type of milk container. We also had a witness from the Environmental Protection Agency from Washington, who testified on behalf of the plaintiffs in the lower court in Minnesota, who testified that there were no federal statutes or regula­ tions contemplated in regard to regulating plastic containers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No one knows really very much about this problem as yet, do we? Scientists, environmentalists, govern­ ments, it's really -- we're going into a fog bank, aren't we, to a large extent?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: We did have, of course, the MRI report in the lower court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I was speaking of experience. There's very little experience to go on, isn't that true?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Very little experience in this type of statute, Your Honor, yes. And also I would agree that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The technology?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Technology? Right. Because we talk this matter. They used to be flared off; they're now being used in this country for petrochemicals. The Minnesota court found two basic factors. One, both courts found there was no rational nexus between what the State of Minnesota said it wanted to accomplish, that is, environmental purpose, and the means selected to accomplish it, that is, banning this container of one liquid.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose that at the time the legislation was passed the industry was using plastic and paper half and half, and the Minnesota court said, well, we want to get to plastic. Wouldn't that be rationally connected with it?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: We submit, not on the evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it may not be. I didn't ask whe­ ther it would be constitutional. I just said —

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Would it be rationally --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- would it be rationally connected? If it prevented half the nonreturnables, I suppose it's rationally connected, isn't it? One, plastic has been there for ten or twelve years. Second, the second —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, you're back in my first ques­ tion, then.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: All right. That is, that the trial court found after taking voluminous evidence that in Minnesota there would be no return to returnables. If plastic is abandoned, the housewife and the dairy would go only to the paperboard, nonrefillable, nonreturnable container. The evidence -- that was a finding by the trial court upon the evidence in this moting refillables, it just as a practical matter does not do it. Furthermore, the Department of Agriculture of Minnesota is opposed to refillables. The City of Rochester would not permit refillables because of the fact that the

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They would have shifted to glass or some- thing.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, why can't the Legislature say, half at a time?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Because the half that they are going to take out isn't going to accomplish the purpose of getting refillables.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would be one step, though. You can't

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Not necessarily, when -- 100 percent of the consumers would then go to the paperboard, as the evidence showed. You wouldn't get refillables. They didn't get them in Canada in Ontario, where they banned both. In Canada they go to the polyethylene nonrefillable soft plastic. The house- wife will not go to refillables. paper, you would suggest that everybody would have gone to plastic? half-gallon, is pretty unsatisfactory?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: If there was, Mr. Justice Stevens, I don't recall it. However, I do believe that, obviously, there was conflicting evidence on many points in this case, and the court did make a determination on what it considered to be the great preponderance of the evidence. The Minnesota courts found, again -- if there is -- plastic is banned, there is no return to refillables, and the milk market will be preempted by paperboard. There are no plans as I indicated for federal or State additional regula­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if the legislative findings had in so many words found that, if we ban plastic, there will be a substantial switch to returnables?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In what case is that? HR. KEYES: I would say in the Hunt v. --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the Apple case?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: The Apple case. A New Jersey case, Philadelphia v. New Jersey. And in Craig v. Boren, and in Raymond Motor Freight, all of which, the state had an ostensi­ ble purpose. And the trial court found on the basis of volumi­ nous evidence that that purpose was not being effected by the statute. And because of that, this Court found, or agreed, that the mere statement --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what is the standard? That there isn't any rational basis for the legislative finding, or that, although it may be rational, we find the facts to the contrary? What is the standard?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Well, the standard as I understand it -- — and I think the standard our Supreme Court used -- is there was no rational basis for the Legislature to have conceived that these means would bring about the end that they sought to bring about. And I believe the Dukes standard on equal leaching of the surface waters through the vegetable matter into the underground aquifers which are so important in our state, and I'm sure in most state; no methane gas. In fact, many — the evidence showed that many landfills are lined with plastic to stop the leaching. Furthermore, you have stable landfills. As I indicated earlier, plastic is completely re- cycleable and completely combustible. None of those are pro­ perties of the so-called paperboard container, which incident­ ally is coated with plastic; not the wax that you used to get years ago, it's plastic-coated, melded. plastic containers are as environmentally sound as paperboard in a number of other matters, and in no case is it inferior. Therefore they found that the statute is really not an environ­ the statute is for the purpose of -- the lower court: insu­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then how do we get it? got the Barchi, when an issue was not litigated in Barchi, you asked that it be briefed. Beazer — New York City Transit Authority v. Beazer, where the lower trial court decided a case on the constitutional issue, filing the statutory issues, and the circuit court, if I recall, addressed only one of the issues, and you decided on the statutory issues.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you rely on the Commerce Clause issue in both courts below?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: We did. We relied on it in both courts below, we briefed it in both courts. We briefed it in our opposition to cert, in this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then you won in the District Court on all your grounds, didn't you?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: We won on all grounds except that the District Court found that the due process, substantive due process and equal protection were both based on the rationality nexus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: And so it didn't address it too well.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Solely on Equal Protection.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there an Equal Protection Clause in the Minnesota Constitution?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: There is, Your Honor, but there is no Commerce Clause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that. So -- but you didn't present any State grounds?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: We did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why didn't you?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Because the two clauses are identical,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know, but if you'd have won on the State ground, they could never have come here.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: That would have been -- we didn't under­ that -- suppliers, fabricators, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They're not the dairies?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Not the dairies; no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, the dairies are parties to this litigation?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: They are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So this argument isn't really advanced on their behalf?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: It's on their behalf to the extent that they fabricate plastic milk bottles in Minnesota. Some of the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they want to sell their --

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They want to sell their milk in plastic containers, don't they?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Yes. Yes, they do. the right to sell milk in paper containers but not in plastic containers. Aren’t all dairies being treated alike?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Not to the extent that the plastic con­ tainer, we feel, or at least the evidence showed, was a much more attractive container and permits the sale --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I understand you think it's better and it's irrational. I'm just addressing myself to the equal protection theory.. Are there any cases that you know of in which all businesses within the State are treated alike, it's plastics business, whatever it might be. Does that violate the Equal Protection Clause?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: Not internally. However, milk is com­ peting at all times with many other, with soft drinks and many other liquids. And in that case the package or container, we b e- lieve, does assist the sale of milk by these dairies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I understand that. I asked if there is any precedent for holding that denying a business the right to engage in a particular kind of activity violates the Equal Protection Clause?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: It does when there's no rational basis nexus for the discrimination generally speaking.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your answer is, no, you don't have any cases for that proposition?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: I don't have any cases --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Keyes, what's the competition between gallons of milk and soft drinks? MR. KEYES What is the -- ? I'm sorry, I didn't understand.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The competition between a gallon con- tainer of milk and soft drinks? MR. KEYES The only —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They're both liquids? MR. KEYES They're both liquids, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what else? MR. KEYES That's -- the dairies, I believe, find that in order to compete in the liquid, consumer market, they should have an attractive package and should be entitled was no political problem whatsoever with the banning of out- of-state interests. But the local interests such as the can manufacturers and others who would be affected by an effective litter or container deposit bill were not affected by this legislation, because politically, they could not have been.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What out-of-state industry as of the pre­ sent time, or at the time of beginning of the litigation, was being banned?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: The plastic industry.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: No, no, only in regard to the making of milk bottles, plastic containers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I understood the State to say that they were going to set up a plant in Minnesota, and that this was what brought up, one of the things that brought up the legislation.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: I was very unaware of that, and it never has happened. There id no there are fabricating plants' in' Minnesota which submit, for interstate commerce purposes, is between producers of milk containers. We further submit that the movement of plastic containers, raw material, equipment, is halted at the border of Minnesota. It can't get into the State for the pur­ pose of retail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, this is your interstate commerce argument ?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: This is our interstate commerce., sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That you're making as an alternative ground to support the judgment in your favor in the Minnesota Supreme Court?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: That is correct, Mr. Justice Stewart. The evidence was clear that Phillips Petroleum would lase about a half a million dollars annually as a result of this statute. The Hoover Company has already lost a million and a half dol­ lars and it would be absolutely foreclosed from the Minnesota market. The Wells Dairy in Le Mars, Iowa, would not be able to ship about a million gallons of plastic-contained milk into Minnesota if this statute were permitted.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: On Commerce; yes. That's correct. The legislative history in this case indicates that the timber interests were to be protected. It's the third largest employer in our State -- that was in the legislative history. The plastic industry was referred to by the author of' the bill as the boys in the alligator shoes from New York and Chicago who were going to come in our State and tell us how to run it; no importation of competing petrochemicals or plastics from out-of-state. Further, this Court has stated that protec­ tionism can arise from the effect of a statute as well as from a protectionist legislative motion. Very few legislatures state exactly that they are going to cast an undue burden on commerce. But the practical effect of the statute -- and which it is here -- no chemical industry in Minnesota, no plastic industry; there is an extensive timber industry. This Court is aware of a number of recent cases by hit, just in regard to this point. The evidence convinced the Minnesota court that the purported State goal was environmental protection, was illusory. At least the trial court found that. And that's the same result as this Court determined in regard to Raymond Motor This Court after an examination of the evidence found in the contrary. Philadelphia v. New Jersey, environment and public health were going to be supported by this statute. This Court after review of the evidence found that it was illusory. A slight or non-existent legitimate state purpose cannot justify a burden on commerce, even though the burden is slight. That is what I believe this Court has said in these matters, and that is the basis of our Commerce Clause argument. The Minnesota court also found that Equal Protection was violated on the basis of no rational nexus. I am aware of the three-tiered approach used by this Court in that matter and of course if the approach should be abandoned, we sub­ mit that Minnesota's justification of the statute is insuffi­ cient to support the discrimination between milk containers. There's just no -- whether it's rational basis or whether it's substantial state purpose. Even if the Minnesota end be legitimate, the Court held that the means selected did not effect an appropriate by step basis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As long as it's a step.

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: As long as it's a step. And our Supreme Court —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But this is a non-step, you say?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: That's what our Supreme Court said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the District Court?

turns.name: KEYES
turns.text: And the District Court. And again, on the basis that a journey of a thousand miles begins with one foot forward, is valid only if that foot is forward, we submit, We submit that that is what our court held on the basis of voluminous findings of fact, that the step is not a forward step, and because of that the statute cannot be justified under the Commerce Clause, we submit, and also as the Minnesota Supreme Court determined, under the Equal Protection Clause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the State ever argue that as a matter enactments cannot be examined by the courts. They lost that in the District Court. They argued it again in the District Court and in the Supreme Court. The Minnesota court held that the courts could not and should not abandon their role to de­ termine the constitutionality of statutes. That is simply what they held.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, mightn't you get a different answer to that question in terms of whether you're relying on the Federal Constitution or the State? that we would. I do know that our Minnesota court was cer­ tainly aware of the fact that the courts are not super-legisla­ tures and that they have a distinctive role in the constitu­ tionality of statutes. And they exercised it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Keyes. Mr. Raschke, do you have anything further? You have a couple of minutes left.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Just a couple of further comments, State constitutional provision to base their equal protection argument in the complaint. Secondly, Mr. Keyes mentioned that plastic burns at a higher BTU value. There's a very good reason for that. It has a whole lot more energy locked up in it. The trial court found that it has three times, roughly, the energy that the paperboard container has in it. With respect to the question of protecting the pulpwood industry, there's no evidence on record that demon­ strates that these paperboard containers actually come from any Minnesota-paper products as opposed to products of any one of a large number of other states which are engaged in the paper industry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would that be a matter outside judicial notice? I suppose you would suggest it is.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Pardon me, your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that a matter outside of judicial notice or — I suppose you'd say it is.

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: Well, I don't -- if you're asking me numerous cases involving paper -- say they were judicially noticing this production and resource factor? Did its opinion mention that?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: With respect to discrimination-- paper?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, did they refer to the fact that -- the source of these containers, whether they were made inside or outside the State?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: The Act makes no distinction whatsoever with respect to where the containers, raw materials, or any­ thing else came --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did the District Court find?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: The State Supreme Court found --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did the District Court find?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: -- with respect to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did the District Court find?

turns.name: RASCHKE
turns.text: I don't think there was any finding with respect to where the paper for the paperboard container comes from, whether within or without the State. Certainly the statute doesn't make that distinction. Certainly the The case is submitted.
